metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the env var or config key (e.g. `REBAR_OPCERT_REVIEW_REMOTE_URL`) that tells the server worker which git remote URL to fetch `main` from, and add it to the Scope 'In' list alongside the other named env vars.
findings.finding: The server must git-fetch 'the review remote's main tip' but no configuration key or env var is named for specifying the review remote URL — an executor cannot implement the authoritative fetch without inventing this.
findings.location: What section — 'Authoritative fetch' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add `REBAR_OPCERT_REVIEW_REMOTE_URL` (string, required) to the What section's worker description alongside `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`, and note it in the Scope 'In' list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the env var name the server reads to obtain the expected X-Opcert-Guard secret value (e.g. `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD_TOKEN`) and note it as a required server config item.
findings.finding: The guard secret that the server validates from the `X-Opcert-Guard` header has no named env var or config source specified, so an executor cannot wire the server-side validation without inventing the mechanism.
findings.location: What section — 'Stateless / store-read-only' bullet, X-Opcert-Guard description
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The server reads the expected secret from `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD_TOKEN` (required env var; startup fails if absent).' to the X-Opcert-Guard description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC asserting that a request to `POST /opcert/jobs` (or any endpoint) with a missing or incorrect `X-Opcert-Guard` header is rejected with a 4xx, and that a correct header is accepted — covering the described defense-in-depth control.
findings.finding: The `X-Opcert-Guard` header check (described in 'What' as a defense-in-depth authn layer) is a concrete deliverable with no covering acceptance criterion — no AC asserts that requests missing or carrying an invalid guard header are rejected.
findings.location: What — 'the opcert app checks an `X-Opcert-Guard` **header** instead'; Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] Guard-header enforcement: a request to `POST /opcert/jobs` with no `X-Opcert-Guard` header (or a wrong value) returns 401/403; a request with the correct header proceeds normally. The header value is read from the same configuration mechanism as `review_bot`\'s `?token=` check.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `review_plan(sign=True)` vs. store-read-only contradiction: the plan must either (a) specify that the service calls `review_plan(..., sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)` and explain why that is safe given the seam signs separately, or (b) accept that the service does write the HMAC SIGNATURE event and remove / revise the 'server writes NOTHING' constraint and AC 5.
findings.finding: The plan requires the service to be 'stateless / store-read-only' and that 'the server appends no tickets-branch event', yet it also says the worker dispatches `rebar.llm.review_plan(...)` without passing `sign=False`. In the actual codebase, `review_plan` calls `sign_plan_review` → `signing.sign_manifest` → `append_event` on a PASS verdict — which atomically appends a SIGNATURE event to the ticket's event log and pushes it to the tickets branch. These two constraints are mechanically incompatible: the plan cannot have both 'call review_plan with default sign=True' and 'the server writes NOTHING to the tickets branch'. A PASS gate run would write a SIGNATURE event regardless of the opcert envelope, silently violating the read-only invariant the plan's AC 5 tests for.
findings.location: What section — 'Stateless / store-read-only'; 'the handler itself never calls a sign function and never passes sign=False'; AC 5 'the server appends no tickets-branch event'
findings.suggested_fix: Call `rebar.llm.review_plan(ticket_id, ..., sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)` in the worker (the producer seam mints the opcert envelope; the HMAC signing path is redundant and its store write breaks the read-only contract). Update AC 5 to assert that no SIGNATURE event is appended (which then holds) and add a rationale sentence explaining that the seam — not the handler — is the sole signer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the value vocabulary for `error.class` in the job response schema (e.g., the Python exception class name string such as `'LLMUnavailableError'`, or a fixed set of string values like `'llm_error'|'llm_unavailable'|'timeout'|'internal'`) so the client command and tests can branch on it.
findings.finding: The job response schema includes `error: {class, message}|null` but the value vocabulary for `error.class` is never defined. The plan does not specify whether this field carries a Python exception class name (e.g. 'LLMUnavailableError'), a fixed string enum, or some other format. An implementor building the service or the client must guess the contract.
findings.location: What section — Response/job schema: `error: {class, message}|null`
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that `error.class` carries the Python exception class name (e.g., `'LLMUnavailableError'`, `'LLMError'`, `'TimeoutError'`) so the client can use it for exit-code routing and log messages.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the format and value vocabulary for `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` (e.g., 'a string like `prod` or `env:prod`; the same value the IaC story will inject; must match what `rebar verify-opcert` checks as the allowed principal') so the key-materialization AC and tests can assert the correct principal.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` as 'the principal for the gate run' set by the worker before dispatching, but never defines what value vocabulary or format a valid principal takes. The signing seam (`sshsig.sign` / DSSE) uses the principal as a binding identity in the signed certificate, making the format security-relevant — yet an implementor cannot determine the correct value to set.
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam, once; the service only PROVISIONS the key' paragraph; also Key materialization AC

findings.finding: The `error` field in the job response schema is specified as `{class, message}|null`, but the vocabulary for `class` is never defined. It is a new field introduced only in this plan. Without knowing whether `class` is the Python exception class name string (e.g. 'LLMUnavailableError'), a fixed enum (e.g. 'llm_unavailable'|'llm_error'), or some other identifier, an implementor cannot write a conformant serializer and the client (`remote-cert`) cannot reliably branch on `error.class` for its 'non-zero exit with the job's error class' behavior.
findings.location: What section — 'Response/job schema' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step (or AC note) to regenerate `docs/env-vars.md` via `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` after introducing `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH`, `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, and `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` under `src/rebar/opcert_service/`, to satisfy the existing env-var registry drift gate enforced by `tests/unit/test_gen_env_registry.py`.
findings.finding: Three new env vars read under src/rebar (REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH, REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID, REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) will trip the existing CI drift gate (scripts/gen_env_registry.py → docs/env-vars.md), but the plan has no AC or step that mentions regenerating the registry — 'make test' will fail on this gate.
findings.location: What section (key materialization / REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH + REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID + REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS); AC: 'make lint && make typecheck && make test green'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the implementation checklist: 'After adding env reads, run `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and commit the updated `docs/env-vars.md`.' Alternatively, add a note to the final AC that `make test` includes this drift gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test) that asserts a job whose gate run exceeds `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` transitions to `status='error'` with an appropriate `error.class` (e.g., `TimeoutError`), and that the background worker continues processing subsequent jobs after a timeout.
findings.finding: The async job timeout path (REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS exceeded during a running job) is a user-facing terminal state of the new job lifecycle API, but no AC specifies what status/fields the job record should contain when the timeout fires, and no test is required to exercise this path.
findings.location: AC list — no AC covers the timeout-exceeded path; 'What' section: 'Single background worker drains serially under a bounded per-run timeout: default 900 s (… configurable via REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC list: 'A test configures REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=1, submits a job backed by a gate stub that sleeps > 1 s, and asserts GET /opcert/jobs/{id} eventually returns status="error" with error.class containing "Timeout"; a second job submitted afterwards completes successfully (worker liveness after timeout).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare how the X-Opcert-Guard expected secret is stored and injected into the service container (e.g., SSM SecureString fetched at startup like `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN`, never in a committed file), and add it to the Scope or the service config section.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that API Gateway injects the X-Opcert-Guard static header, but never declares how the expected guard-secret value is held inside the service container — whether as a plaintext environment variable, an SSM-sourced secret, or another mechanism. An undeclared storage posture for this secret on the boundary-crossing path is a secret-lifecycle gap.
findings.location: What > Service > Stateless / store-read-only paragraph ('the opcert app checks an X-Opcert-Guard header')
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the review_bot pattern: add an `OPCERT_GUARD_SECRET` entry to the SSM fetch script and read it via `os.environ` in the service config, explicitly stating this in the plan. Reference the same SSM-sourced pattern used for `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN`.

findings.finding: The shared-secret header used as defense-in-depth is not named (no header name), and the plan does not specify how its value is provisioned in the server container (env var? SSM? which name?), leaving the secret's lifecycle on the boundary-crossing path undeclared.
findings.location: What > Service > Stateless / store-read-only bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Stateless / store-read-only' bullet add: 'The shared-secret is read from env var OPCERT_TOKEN (SSM-sourced at deploy time); the header is X-Rebar-Token; an empty/unset OPCERT_TOKEN causes the service to return 401 for every request (fail-closed, mirroring review_bot's webhook_token guard).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that a non-PASS verdict returned (not raised) from the gate function (BLOCK, INDETERMINATE, FAIL) maps to `status="completed"` with the native `verdict` field set — distinguishing it from the `status="error"` path reserved for raised LLMError exceptions.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the job status produced when `rebar.llm.review_plan(...)` returns a non-PASS verdict (BLOCK or INDETERMINATE) via normal return — as opposed to raising LLMError. The plan only says 'A raised LLMError/LLMUnavailableError maps to the job's transport status', leaving the BLOCK/INDETERMINATE completion-path ambiguous: is the job status `"completed"` (with `verdict="BLOCK"` or `verdict="INDETERMINATE"`) or `"error"`? This is a processing-protocol gap adjacent to the job schema.
findings.location: What section — Per-kind dispatch + native verdicts; AC line 3 (per-kind verdict mapping AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'Any non-exception gate outcome (BLOCK, INDETERMINATE, FAIL) yields status="completed" with the native verdict string; only a raised LLMError/LLMUnavailableError yields status="error" with verdict=null.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and name the library (e.g., `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `urllib3`/`httpx`, or `requests-aws4auth`) that will provide SigV4 request signing for `rebar remote-cert`, declare it in a new `opcert` (or `reviewbot`) extra in `pyproject.toml`, and confirm it does not land in the stdlib-only core.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `rebar remote-cert` client 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials' but names no library, module, or dependency that provides SigV4 request signing. No SigV4 signing utility exists anywhere in the codebase (no botocore.auth usage for request signing, no requests_aws4auth, no aws_sigv4, no awscrt), and no opcert extra or new dependency is declared in pyproject.toml. This is an unverified capability assertion on a security-critical path.
findings.location: What > Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` (already a transitive of boto3, which is in the reviewbot extra) to sign an `AWSPreparedRequest`-compatible structure, or declare `requests-aws4auth` in a new `opcert` extra. Document the chosen approach in the plan's Client section and add it to the Scope/dependency list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific library and pyproject.toml extra that provides SigV4 request signing for `rebar remote-cert` (e.g. `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` — already a transitive dep via boto3 — or a dedicated extra), and confirm the credential-chain and region-resolution approach.
findings.finding: The client command `rebar remote-cert` is asserted to 'SigV4-sign the request with ambient AWS credentials', but the plan names no library, module, or pyproject.toml extra that provides this capability. No SigV4 signing library (e.g. botocore.auth.SigV4Auth, requests-aws4auth, httpx-auth-awssigv4) is cited, and none appears in the current pyproject.toml extras or in any existing rebar client code.
findings.location: Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Client section: 'Uses `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` (transitive via boto3; add `boto3` to a new `opcert` extra or reuse the `reviewbot` extra) with `botocore.credentials.get_credentials()` and region from `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` / IMDS.' This avoids a new dep if boto3 is already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Parameterize the SSM key path (e.g., `REBAR_OPCERT_SSM_KEY_PATH` env var defaulting to `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) so dev/staging deployments resolve a non-prod key without code changes.
findings.finding: The SSM path `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is written as a literal string in the service description with no environment-specific parameterization. A dev or staging deployment of the service would hit the production signing key (or fail with an access-denied if IAM is scoped to prod, silently breaking the test environment).
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam, once; the service only PROVISIONS the key' paragraph; SSM path literal `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `REBAR_OPCERT_SSM_KEY_PATH: str = "/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key"` to the service's config model (alongside `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`); the worker reads from the env var rather than a literal. Mention this in the Scope section and in the key-materialization AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare that `GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id}` is also protected by the `X-Opcert-Guard` header (or provide a public-with-rationale access declaration), and add an AC or test asserting a request without the header returns 401/403.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'the app additionally requires a shared secret' (X-Opcert-Guard) but does not state that this auth check applies to GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} — only the POST path is named. GET job records contain verdict, envelope (the signed opcert), and bound fields; if the GET endpoint is unguarded, a lower-trust actor who guesses a job_id can retrieve a signed op-cert without authenticating.
findings.location: What > Service > Stateless / store-read-only paragraph; AC line 'GET /opcert/jobs/{id} reaches a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Stateless/store-read-only paragraph: 'All routes except /health require the X-Opcert-Guard header; a missing or incorrect value returns 401.' Add a corresponding test to the auth AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default client polling interval and max-wait (e.g. 'poll every 10 s up to 960 s, then exit non-zero with a timeout error') so the executor has a concrete target.
findings.finding: The `rebar remote-cert` client 'polls to a terminal status' but no polling interval or client-side timeout is specified, leaving an executor to invent both values.
findings.location: Client section — 'submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The client polls `GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id}` every 10 s up to `REBAR_OPCERT_CLIENT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` (default 960) seconds, then exits non-zero with class `Timeout`.' to the Client section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the client-side polling timeout (e.g. a configurable ceiling matching or exceeding the 900 s server timeout), the retry/backoff policy for transient network errors during polling, and the user-visible message/exit when those paths are taken (include the `job_id` in the output so the user can re-query without re-submitting).
findings.finding: The `rebar remote-cert` client's polling behavior on server-side job timeout (900 s ceiling reached, job → `status=error`) and on client-side network failure during the polling loop is unspecified: no client-side poll timeout, no retry policy, no recovery guidance, and no user-visible message shape are named.
findings.location: Client section ('rebar remote-cert … polls to a terminal status') and AC 1 ('reaches a terminal status via the background worker')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Client section: 'The poller retries transient HTTP errors up to N times with exponential backoff; if no terminal status is observed within REBAR_OPCERT_POLL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS (default 960, slightly above the server ceiling), the client exits non-zero printing the job_id so the user can re-query or re-submit.' Add a matching AC: 'a test asserts the client exits non-zero with the job_id when the poll timeout elapses without a terminal status.'

findings.finding: The client polling behavior has no specified timeout or abandonment path: the plan says the client 'polls to a terminal status' but never specifies a maximum poll duration, backoff strategy, or what happens if the job remains in 'queued'/'running' indefinitely (e.g., worker crash, network partition mid-poll). A developer running `rebar remote-cert` could hang forever with no feedback.
findings.location: Client section: `src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py` description; AC (none of the 8 items address client-side timeout)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `verify.opcert_poll_timeout_seconds` config key (default e.g. 300s) and document that `rebar remote-cert` exits non-zero with a clear 'timed out waiting for job <id>' message if the job does not reach a terminal status within that window. Add an AC: 'a client-side poll that exceeds the configured timeout exits non-zero with a human-readable timeout message.'

findings.finding: The `rebar remote-cert` client's polling strategy (interval, backoff, maximum total wait) is unspecified; an executor must choose these values without guidance.
findings.location: Client section — 'submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Client bullet: 'Polls every 2 s up to 360 s; exits non-zero with `TIMEOUT` error class if no terminal status is reached.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a maximum asyncio.Queue depth (or an explicit rationale for unbounded), and specify the HTTP response (e.g. 503 with Retry-After) when the queue is at capacity.
findings.finding: The in-process asyncio.Queue has no stated maximum depth, and no backpressure or rejection policy (e.g. HTTP 429 / 503 when the queue is full) is defined. With jobs taking 30 s–15 min each and a single serial worker, a burst of submissions causes unbounded queue growth.
findings.location: ## What — 'Async job API … enqueues on an in-process asyncio.Queue'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `asyncio.Queue(maxsize=N)` (e.g. N=20 for a developer-scale service) and return HTTP 503 with a `Retry-After` header when `queue.full()` is True at POST time. Document the chosen limit in the plan's 'What' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry-with-backoff (e.g., 3 attempts, exponential) for the SSM SecureString fetch, and surface SSM-specific failures as a distinct `error.class` (e.g., `SSMFetchError`) in the job record.
findings.finding: No retry or backoff is specified for the SSM SecureString fetch (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`). A transient SSM hiccup will immediately fail the job with no recovery, and the plan is silent on whether the error is surfaced to the caller in a distinguishable way (e.g., a specific error class vs a generic job error).
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam' / key materialization paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the SSM call in a retry loop (e.g., `tenacity.retry` with 3 attempts, exponential backoff, jitter); set `error.class = 'SSMFetchError'` on exhaustion so clients and operators can distinguish infra transients from logic errors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-fetch subprocess timeout (e.g., `git fetch --depth=1` with a `timeout` wrapper) and at least one retry for transient failures, with the fetch error surfaced as a distinct `error.class`.
findings.finding: No retry, backoff, or timeout is specified for the authoritative git fetches (tickets branch + review remote main tip). Network or remote-side slowness will block the single background worker for an unbounded duration (short of the 900s job timeout) with no operator visibility.
findings.location: What section — 'Authoritative fetch' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Run `git fetch` via `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` with a per-call timeout (e.g., 60s), retry once on non-zero exit, and set `error.class = 'AuthFetchError'` on final failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE policy for completed job records (e.g., evict records older than N minutes, or cap the completed-job map at a fixed size with LRU eviction).
findings.finding: Completed and errored job records are stored in memory with no eviction policy. For a long-running service, every completed job permanently occupies memory, creating an unbounded accumulation of shared mutable state.
findings.location: What section — 'Job state is in-memory (a restart loses queued jobs; clients re-submit — cert minting is idempotent)'
findings.suggested_fix: Keep a `collections.OrderedDict` capped at 1000 entries (or TTL-expired after 30 minutes) for completed job records; drop the oldest on overflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the process-global env var mutation as a concurrency hazard and either (a) pass key path as a function argument through the signing seam instead of via env var, or (b) document and enforce that only one worker may ever run per process with a runtime assertion/semaphore.
findings.finding: The worker sets process-global environment variables (`REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH`, `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`) during each job run. Even though the worker is currently serial, this is an unacknowledged global-state mutation: any future addition of concurrency (a second worker, a thread, a test running two jobs in parallel) will introduce TOCTOU races, and the plan provides no locking or scoping mechanism.
findings.location: What section — 'sets REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH to that path and REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID as the principal for the gate run'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer passing key path as a direct argument to the signing call rather than mutating os.environ; if env var is required by the seam interface, wrap the set/sign/clear sequence in an asyncio.Lock to prevent future concurrent use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (new `remote-cert` equality-test intercept arm, mirroring the `verify-identity` pattern) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` (help text) to the plan's edit-target list so `rebar remote-cert` is routable.
findings.finding: The plan names `rebar remote-cert` as a new CLI subcommand but does not enumerate `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` or `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` as edit targets. The CLI router in `_cli/__init__.py` requires an explicit equality-test intercept arm for every non-dispatcher subcommand (see the `verify-identity`, `verify-commit-ticket`, `review-plan` arms at lines 391–436), and `known_subcommands()` derives the recognized set entirely from the presence of help text files in `_cli/help/` — a subcommand with neither arm nor help file is rejected as 'unknown subcommand' before dispatch.
findings.location: What / Client section: '`src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py`, CLI `rebar remote-cert <ticket-id> <kind>`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope / 'In' list: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (add intercept arm `if argv and argv[0] == 'remote-cert': from rebar._commands import remote_cert; return remote_cert.cli(argv[1:])`) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` (usage text pinned by golden tests).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` to the plan's Scope/edit-target list for the `verify.opcert_remote_url: str | None = None` field addition to `VerifyConfig`.
findings.finding: `verify.opcert_remote_url` is a new `VerifyConfig` field but `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` is not listed as an edit target anywhere in the plan's Scope or What sections. `VerifyConfig` (lines 209–296 of `_config_schema.py`) currently has no `opcert_remote_url` field; adding it requires editing that file.
findings.location: What / Client section: 'Reads `verify.opcert_remote_url` (new `VerifyConfig` key, `str | None = None`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` is in scope for an `opcert-service` service block (mirroring the review-bot entry); if so, add it to the edit-target list; if intentionally deferred to the IaC story, explicitly say so in the Scope section.
findings.finding: `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` is an unenumerated consumer of the new `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert`. The reviewbot precedent (which the plan explicitly mirrors) has a corresponding `review-bot:` service block in docker-compose.yml. Without a compose entry the new Dockerfile cannot be built or run locally via the existing compose workflow, and the pattern reference is incomplete.
findings.location: What / Container image: 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert (build context = repo root, per the review_bot Dockerfile pattern)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum queue depth for the `asyncio.Queue` (e.g., `maxsize=50`) and define the HTTP response (429 with `Retry-After`) when the queue is full.
findings.finding: The in-memory `asyncio.Queue` has no stated maximum depth. Under a burst of client submissions the queue grows without bound, consuming memory until the process OOMs; there is also no specified back-pressure response (e.g., HTTP 429) to signal clients to back off.
findings.location: What section — 'Async job API' paragraph; 'enqueues on an in-process `asyncio.Queue`'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `asyncio.Queue(maxsize=N)` where N is documented in config (e.g., `REBAR_OPCERT_MAX_QUEUE_DEPTH`, default 50); `POST /opcert/jobs` returns 429 when `queue.full()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement that the SSM key-fetch-to-temp-file path sanitizes exception messages and log statements to never include the raw key bytes, and add a test asserting no key material appears in log output when the temp-file write fails.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString fetch (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) returns raw Ed25519 private-key bytes that are written to a temp file. The plan does not specify that the key material must be suppressed from logs and exception tracebacks on the fetch-and-write path. A broad exception handler that logs the boto3 response or the raw bytes (e.g., `logger.exception(...)` with `exc_info=True` on a boto3 `ClientError`) could emit the key value in plaintext to the container log.
findings.location: What > Service > Signing = the producer seam paragraph ('fetches the SSM SecureString … materializes it to a 0600 mkstemp temp file')
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the SSM value in a redacting wrapper before passing it to the write path, and catch specific boto3 exceptions (logging only the error code, not the response body). State this explicitly in the key-materialization AC or the signing paragraph.

findings.finding: The plan contains a self-contradicting signing protocol that leaves the key-materialization → producer-seam integration sequence unspecified. The 'Signing = the producer seam, once' bullet says 'the handler does NOT sign separately and does NOT pass sign=False', yet the very next sentences in the same bullet describe the handler fetching an Ed25519 key from AWS SSM and materializing it to a 0600 mkstemp temp file for `ssh-keygen -Y sign`. The data-flow connecting (a) SSM fetch → (b) temp file → (c) injection into the producer-seam `sign_opcert_manifest` call is never sequenced. An implementor cannot determine whether the handler sets an environment variable/path before calling `sign_opcert_manifest`, passes the path as an argument, or performs a direct `sshsig.sign` call outside the producer seam.
findings.location: What section — 'Signing = the producer seam, once' bullet; also Key materialization AC
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Signing = the producer seam, once' bullet, add: 'The handler materializes the SSM key to a temp file, then passes the file path to `sign_opcert_manifest(... key_path=<tmp_path> ...)` (the seam's API accepts a key_path argument); the seam function shells out to `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>`. The handler owns the temp-file lifecycle (open → pass path → finally: delete); the seam owns the signing subprocess.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts the opcert service uses 'a shared-secret **header** as defense in depth, matching `review_bot`'s token check', but `review_bot/app.py` implements its secret check via a URL **query parameter** (`request.query_params.get("token", "")`), not an HTTP header. The mirroring claim is contradicted by the actual code.
findings.location: What section: 'the app additionally requires a shared-secret header as defense in depth, matching review_bot's token check'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a shared-secret **header**' with 'a shared-secret **query parameter** (`?token=`)' to accurately describe the review_bot-mirrored pattern; or, if a header is intentional for this service, remove the 'matching review_bot's token check' parenthetical since the mechanisms differ.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC5 into three separate acceptance criteria: (a) store-unchanged assertion, (b) client SIGNATURE event persistence and verify-opcert certification, (c) tampered-envelope rejection — each independently checkable.
findings.finding: AC5 bundles three distinct verifiable behaviors — (1) server writes nothing to the tickets branch, (2) client persists the envelope as a SIGNATURE event, (3) a tampered envelope is rejected — into a single checkbox, making it difficult to confirm each is independently satisfied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (AC5)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md or ADR entry documenting the opcert service's key implementation-level architectural decisions: SSM-to-mkstemp key materialization rationale (ssh-keygen needs a file path, not stdin), X-Opcert-Guard header vs query-param choice (API Gateway static-header injection), and asyncio.Queue serial-worker pattern.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel service architecture — trusted-env fetch, SSM-to-mkstemp key materialization, asyncio.Queue serial worker, and API Gateway + X-Opcert-Guard defense-in-depth — without requiring an ADR or CLAUDE.md update to document these decisions. The epic's 'design resolution' section covers high-level forks but not the implementation-level patterns specific to this story.
findings.location: ## What (service section) and ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/opcert-service-patterns.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section) covering: (1) why mkstemp + finally for key materialization, (2) why X-Opcert-Guard header over query param, (3) why a single serial worker is correct for this workload. Reference it from the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: structured log lines at job state transitions (queued→running→completed/error), distinct log fields for error class (SSM/fetch/LLM/timeout), and at least a log-based alert or metric counter for `status=error` jobs and worker-crash events.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (logging, metrics, or alerting) for any of the new failure points: SSM fetch failures, git fetch failures, LLM errors, worker exceptions, or job timeout expirations. Operators cannot distinguish a hung worker from a healthy idle one.
findings.location: What section — overall service design; no observability mention anywhere in the plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the background worker is supervised (e.g., wrapped in a restart loop or monitored via a health-check endpoint that returns 503 if the worker task is not alive).
findings.finding: The background worker's crash-recovery path is unspecified. If the single `asyncio` worker task raises an unhandled exception (e.g., from an unexpected error in dispatch), it silently exits and no new worker is spawned, leaving the queue permanently drained with no operator signal.
findings.location: What section — 'Single background worker drains serially under a bounded per-run timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the worker coroutine in a `while True` loop with a top-level `except Exception` that logs and re-enters the drain loop; expose a `GET /healthz` that checks `worker_task.done()` and returns 503 if so.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC asserting that `POST /opcert/jobs` with an unrecognized `kind` returns HTTP 422 (or equivalent structured error), and that an empty/missing `ticket_id` is rejected before any job is enqueued.
findings.finding: POST /opcert/jobs with an invalid or unknown `kind` value (not in the "completion-verifier"|"plan-review" enum) and with a missing/empty `ticket_id` are user-facing validation failure paths of the new API endpoint with no AC or test specified.
findings.location: AC1 and 'Request schema' in the 'What' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `sign_opcert_manifest` function signature and the `<tracker>/.opcert-key` key-resolution interface this story extends, either inline or as a reference to a committed (not just installed) artifact, so implementers can verify the contract without accessing out-of-repo Gerrit changes.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `signing.sign_opcert_manifest` is a hard dependency 'not yet on main' (Gerrit changes 649/650/660), yet `resolve_symbol` reports it EXISTS in the installed environment while a grep of `src/rebar/signing.py` (the named file) finds no such function. The plan's scope description also says the seam adds 'REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH takes precedence over the `<tracker>/.opcert-key` genesis' — but neither `opcert-key` genesis logic nor `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` appears anywhere in the current repo. The discrepancy between the installed env and the file-level source means implementers cannot know which interface contract (function signature, key-resolution order) to implement against without consulting the out-of-repo Gerrit changes.
findings.location: Why section — Hard dependencies paragraph; What section — 'Signing = the producer seam' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the plan (or a stub/interface definition in-repo) specifying the exact `rebar.attest.opcert` API surface the service imports (function names, return types for the DSSE envelope), so the round-trip test (AC-1) can be written and run without requiring the out-of-repo Gerrit change to be pre-installed.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `src/rebar/attest/opcert.py` exists as part of the hard-dependency substrate consumed by the service handler, but no such file exists in `src/rebar/attest/` (confirmed by directory listing). The plan delegates to this module for op-cert envelope construction (`rebar.opcert.v1` cert format, DSSE envelope encoding), yet the What section describes behavior that depends on it ('envelope: <encoded DSSE>|null') without specifying what fallback or stub the implementer should use while the dependency is in review.
findings.location: Why section — Hard dependencies paragraph; What section — Response/job schema

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `X-Opcert-Guard` value is sourced from SSM/Secrets Manager (not hardcoded in the API Gateway integration or container env), and add a pointer to this in either this plan's Scope section or the IaC story's scope.
findings.finding: The `X-Opcert-Guard` shared-secret value is injected by API Gateway as a 'static request header', but the plan never states how that secret value is stored or provisioned — if it is hardcoded in the API Gateway integration config (IaC) or in the container environment without referencing a secrets manager, it is a plaintext secret in IaC.
findings.location: What section — 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph; 'the opcert app checks an `X-Opcert-Guard` header instead, because API Gateway injects static request headers on the integration'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum a `GET /health` liveness endpoint and specify that structured job-error events are logged at ERROR level; note whether metrics/alarms are delegated to the IaC story or owned here.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability for the opcert service: no health-check endpoint, no structured error logging, no metrics (job latency, queue depth, error rate), and no alerting on signing failures. For a trusted gate service whose unavailability silently blocks developer workflows, this is an operational gap.
findings.location: What section — 'What' (Service) section; Acceptance Criteria; Scope

findings.finding: `rebar verify-opcert` is referenced in AC5 as an existing certifying command ('persists the returned envelope as a SIGNATURE event that verify_signature/rebar verify-opcert then certify'), but zero occurrences of `verify-opcert` or `verify_opcert` exist anywhere in the codebase (src/, _commands/, _cli/). It is not listed in the story's Scope-In, nor among the declared forward-reference dependencies (Gerrit 649/650/660, which cover sign_opcert_manifest / attest/opcert.py / trusted_env.py / sshsig.sign).
findings.location: AC5: 'rebar remote-cert persists the returned envelope … that verify_signature/rebar verify-opcert then certify'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Hard Dependencies section add `rebar verify-opcert` (the command that certifies a stored DSSE envelope) alongside `sign_opcert_manifest` / `attest/opcert.py`; OR add it to Scope-In and give it its own AC bullet.

findings.finding: AC5 references `rebar verify-opcert` as a command used to certify the returned envelope ('persists the returned envelope ... that `verify_signature`/`rebar verify-opcert` then certify'), but `rebar verify-opcert` does not exist anywhere in the codebase, is not in this story's Scope/In list, and is not listed as a dependency. The only existing verify command is `rebar verify-signature`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'a tampered envelope is rejected (`mismatch`) … `verify_signature`/`rebar verify-opcert` then certify'
findings.suggested_fix: If `rebar verify-signature` already handles DSSE op-cert envelopes via the existing `signing.verify_signature` path, update AC5 to read '`verify_signature`/`rebar verify-signature` then certify'. If a separate op-cert verify command is truly needed, add it to Scope/In.

findings.finding: `rebar verify-opcert` is referenced in AC5 ('a tampered envelope is rejected (mismatch)') as a command the client test uses to certify and tamper-check the returned envelope, but this command does not exist anywhere in the codebase (search for 'verify_opcert' returns zero hits in src/). It is unclear whether `rebar verify-opcert` is in-scope for this story or is a forward reference to an unbuilt dependency, and if it must be built here, no design spec is given for it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, AC5

findings.finding: The background worker's per-run timeout value is described as 'bounded' but no concrete value (seconds/minutes) is given; an executor must invent one, risking a value that is too short (gate runs take '30s–minutes' per the plan) or too long (no upper bound).
findings.location: What section — 'Single background worker drains serially under a bounded per-run timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the worker description: 'Single background worker drains serially under a 300 s per-run timeout (configurable via `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_S`; default 300).'

findings.finding: The 'bounded per-run timeout' for the background worker is mentioned but its value is never specified and is not marked as configurable (e.g. an env var or config key). For a path that drives LLM calls (30s–minutes range cited), this threshold is expected to evolve and should not be silently hardcoded.
findings.location: What > Service — 'Single background worker drains serially under a bounded per-run timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS: int = 300` (or similar) to the service's settings/env model, document its default, and reference it in the worker loop. Mention it in the AC or Scope section.

findings.finding: The plan requires the FastAPI service to enforce a shared-secret header for defense-in-depth, but never specifies how that secret is provisioned or injected into the server environment (SSM parameter? env var? mounted secret?). If injected as a plaintext env var in a task definition or compose file, it violates the secrets-management constraint; if stored in SSM it needs an IAM policy grant that is also unspecified here.
findings.location: What → Service → 'Stateless / store-read-only' paragraph; AC (no explicit AC line)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The shared-secret token is stored as an SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-shared-secret`) and injected into the container via ECS task-definition `secrets` (never a plaintext env var); the IAM task role is granted `ssm:GetParameter` on that path — specified in the IaC story.' This mirrors the Ed25519 key pattern already in the plan.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
